narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 46.3% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 2, middle family income bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average undergraduate GPA is 3.24 and LSAT is 37.27, while successful full-time students make up 93% of passers, so your profile aligns in some expected ways but stands out in others. The three biggest factors driving this prediction were your LSAT score, your Year 3 decile ranking, and your Year 1 decile ranking. Your LSAT score was the most influential, working strongly in your favor by suggesting a solid potential to pass the bar, much like it does for many admitted students. However, your Year 3 decile ranking of 2 pushed the prediction toward failure more than any other factor, as it places you below the average of about 6 for bar passers, signaling potential challenges in sustaining performance. Your Year 1 decile ranking of 2 also contributed toward the failure prediction, though with less force, again deviating from the higher averages seen in successful students. Compared to typical admitted applicants—who often have deciles around 6, stronger GPAs, and similar LSATs—your early and mid-year rankings were the key gaps that tipped the scales, even with your full-time commitment and middle-income background fitting common patterns. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven estimate based on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score further or strengthening your academic deciles through additional coursework or graduate prep programs, which could shift future predictions in your favor. Many students in similar positions improve these metrics and succeed on reapplication—reach out to admissions for targeted advice on building a stronger profile. With focused effort, you can align more closely with the profiles of those who pass the bar.
